text: The robotic surgical procedure involves repairing bilateral incarcerated inguinal hernias. The surgeon works from lateral to medial, moving from Bogros' space towards the supravesicular space. On the right side, a direct and small indirect hernia are visible, indicating a pantaloon hernia. On the left side, a small indirect hernia with a cord lipoma is observed.

The surgeon carefully maintains a thin flap while exposing both a small indirect and direct hernia. The flap is taken down as one piece, allowing mesh overlap in the center for better access. The surgeon works on the anti-sac, visible as a white structure in the background, stripping fat from its surface.

The surgeon pulls on the inferior portion of the flap, avoiding entry into the hernia sac. Using a lateral oblique camera angle, the surgeon applies cautery to dissect the flap, staying thin and high, medial to the iliac vein. The medial portion of the hernia sac is stripped down from the direct angle hernia space.

The surgeon navigates around the corner, staying close to the vas deferens. They proceed with the medial dissection towards the contralateral side, ensuring clearance past the symphysis pubis. The obturator plug is pulled down during this process.

The surgeon carefully dissects the flap on the left side, pushing fat laterally to cover the nerves, and approaches the deep internal ring. The internal ring appears wide and weakened, revealing a small indirect inguinal hernia and a small cord lipoma.
